title: Elastic, Highly Available Web Tier
description:
## Surviving an AZ Outage Under Variable Load

A company runs a **stateless** web/application tier on EC2 in a single Availability Zone behind a single large instance. Traffic is highly variable: quiet overnight, with sharp, unpredictable spikes during the day. The business now requires the tier to **stay available even if an entire Availability Zone fails** and to **scale automatically** with load while keeping cost reasonable during quiet periods. Session state has already been externalized to DynamoDB, so any instance can serve any request.

Which architecture BEST meets these requirements?

options.text: Replace the instance with a much larger instance type and add a CloudWatch alarm that emails the team when CPU is high so they can resize it manually
Put the instances in an EC2 Auto Scaling group spanning at least two Availability Zones behind an Application Load Balancer, using a target-tracking scaling policy on average CPU/request count
Keep a single instance but enable an Elastic IP and a standby instance in the same AZ that you start manually if the primary fails
Run the instance in one AZ and configure Route 53 latency-based routing to a copy in a second AZ that you keep permanently running at full size

explanation: A stateless tier that must survive an AZ failure and scale with load is the canonical use case for an **Auto Scaling group across multiple AZs behind an Application Load Balancer**. The ASG spread across 2+ AZs gives high availability (if one AZ fails, capacity is launched/served in the others), and a **target-tracking** policy automatically adds/removes capacity to hold a metric (e.g. average CPU or ALB request count per target) near a target value — elasticity without manual intervention. Option A (vertical scaling) does not address AZ failure and requires manual, slow human response. Option C is single-AZ and manual, so it fails the HA requirement. Option D is single-AZ for the primary, keeps a full-size copy running permanently (no elasticity, higher cost), and relies on DNS rather than load balancing across a fleet.

hints.title: What two requirements are coupled?
hints.body: You need both fault tolerance across AZs AND automatic elasticity. Only one option delivers both without manual steps.

hints.title: Vertical vs horizontal
hints.body: A bigger single box (scale up) never survives losing the AZ that box lives in. Surviving an AZ outage requires capacity in another AZ (scale out).
